Name: compressive_learning
Title: Operational Bounds for Neural Network Compression via Rate-Distortion Theory
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that by formulating neural network compression as a rate-distortion problem, we can establish operational bounds on the compressibility of neural networks, providing a fundamental limit on the trade-off between model size and predictive performance.
Related Work: While existing work on neural network compression has focused on empirical methods such as pruning and quantization, our approach is distinct in its emphasis on information-theoretic limits and rate-distortion theory. By leveraging tools from coding theory and statistics, we can derive achievable bounds and converse results that characterize the fundamental limits of compressive learning.
Abstract: This project proposes to investigate the operational bounds of neural network compression via rate-distortion theory. By formulating compression as a lossy source coding problem, we can establish fundamental limits on the trade-off between model size and predictive performance. Our approach combines tools from coding theory, statistics, and machine learning to derive achievable bounds and converse results, providing a principled understanding of the compressibility of neural networks. We will evaluate our approach using distortion-rate frontiers and code efficiency metrics, with a focus on characterizing the information-theoretic limits of compressive learning.
Experiments: Derive achievable bounds for neural network compression using rate-distortion theory
Develop converse results to establish fundamental limits on compressibility
Evaluate the performance of compressed models using distortion-rate frontiers and code efficiency metrics
Compare the results with existing empirical methods for neural network compression
Risk Factors and Limitations: The complexity of neural networks may limit the applicability of our approach
The choice of distortion measure and source distribution may impact the results
The development of efficient coding schemes for neural networks may be challenging
